__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_0__: SaaS MRR Decline Analysis — Buyer-Side Revenue Decay Detection | Churn Lens
__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_16__: Revenue decay is the #1 hidden risk in SaaS acquisitions. Learn how to detect it, measure it, and price it into your offer — or walk away before you overpay.
__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_17__: MRR is the vital sign of any SaaS business. But a flat or even growing MRR can mask alarming underlying decay. Churn Lens analyzes trailing 12 months of subscription data to reveal the real revenue trajectory — with and without new sales.
__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_18__: The Three Types of Revenue Decay
__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_1__: How to detect MRR decline and revenue decay in SaaS acquisition targets. Free tool analyzes trailing 12-month MRR trends, contraction rates, and churn-adjusted projections.
__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_20__: MRR that's flat for 6+ months with no seasonal explanation is the most obvious decay signal. But even "growing" MRR can be decaying — see #3 below.
__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_22__: Newer customer cohorts that churn faster than older cohorts indicate product-market fit erosion, onboarding degradation, or competitive pressure. This is a leading indicator — it shows up 6–12 months before MRR starts dropping.
__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_24__: The most dangerous form of decay. Gross churn runs at 5% monthly, but new sales at 7% monthly makes MRR look like it's growing 2%. The business is actually a leaky bucket that runs on a treadmill: stop adding new customers and the revenue collapses. Churn Lens computes the "organic MRR" — the MRR you'd have without any new sales — to expose this.
__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_26__: A $2.1M ARR target showed 3% month-over-month MRR growth. Churn Lens computed organic MRR: the company was losing 4.2% of revenue every month to churn and contraction. Without $73K/month in new sales, the business would lose half its revenue in 14 months. The buyer re-priced the deal with a 2.1× multiple instead of 4.5×.
__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_29__: — month-by-month MRR with linear regression slope, presented in a visual chart
__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_2__: SaaS MRR Decline Analysis — Buyer-Side Revenue Decay Detection
__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_31__: — what MRR would be in 12 months without new sales, computed from current churn and contraction rates
__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_33__: — revenue lost to downgrades and plan reductions, expressed as a percentage of starting MRR
__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_35__: — retention rate by acquisition cohort (quarterly), with trend line
__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_37__: — the real acquisition multiple when you discount for expected revenue decay post-close
__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_39__: These decay signals should trigger a hard pause:

__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_41__: — the business is structurally shrinking and new sales can't keep pace
__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_42__: Cohort retention is worsening for 4+ consecutive quarters
__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_43__: — this is a structural product/market problem, not a seasonal blip
__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_45__: — more revenue is lost to downgrades than gained from upsells
__TR_saas-mrr-decline-analysis_47__: — the business is a treadmill, not an asset
